coredata.dc:title: Band structures of blue luminescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots by synchrotron-based XPS
coredata.prism:issueName: Special Issue Dedicated to the 75 birthday of Peter R Norton
coredata.dc:creator: He, Shuijian
Turnbull, Matthew J.
Nie, Yuting
Sun, Xuhui
Ding, Zhifeng
coredata.dc:description: This paper is dedicated to Professor P.R. Norton on the occasion of his 75th birthday, in honor of his profound contributions to Surface Science. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) attracted great interests attributed to their outstanding photoluminescence properties. Nitrogen doping is an effective strategy to engineer the optical and electron transfer properties of GQDs. Nitrogen doped GQDs (N-GQDs) were obtained by using cow milk as the precursor via a facile hydrothermal process with a Teflon-lined autoclave heated at 200 °C for 4.5 h. The purified N-GQDs were discovered to have high crystallinity with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to that of in-plane graphene (100 facet). The optical band gap of the N-GQDs was estimated from the Tauc plot derived from their UV–vis absorption spectrum. The full valence band spectrum of N-GQDs was collected and analyzed by means of synchrotron-based X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy for the first time. Corresponding full band structures were elucidated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbogenic quantum dots
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Band gap
Valence and conduction bands
Band structures
Synchrotron spectroscopy

xocs:title-editors-group.ce:title: Special Issue Dedicated to the 75 birthday of Peter R Norton

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Canada Foundation for Innovation/Ontario Innovation Trust
Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada
Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC, DG RGPIN-2013-201697 and SPG STPGP-2016-493924 ), Canada Foundation for Innovation/Ontario Innovation Trust (CFI/OIT, 9040 ), Premier's Research Excellence Award (PREA, 2003 ), and The University of Western Ontario. We appreciate the beam times provided by the Canadian Light Source (CLS), help of the facilities and beamline scientists, and, in particular, guidance of Dr. Xiaoyu Cui throughout the experimentation. We would like to thank the Electronic Shop, Glass Shop and ChemBio Store in the Department of Chemistry for their quality service. We are also grateful to Dr. Zhiqiang Wang for his assistance prior to our CLS visit, and Dr. T.K. Sham for his long-term guidance with regards to all things on synchrotron.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A typical TEM image of the as-prepared N-GQDs (A) and the size distribution histogram (B). The inset of (A) is a HRTEM picture and shows the crystalline lattice fringes of a single N-GQD. The red circles in dash show where N-GQDs are. To collect enough data for the size distribution histogram, the diameters of 103 N-GQDs are recorded.

(A) UV−vis absorption spectrum (the insets show the photographs of N-GQDs solution under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right)). (B) (αhυ)2 vs. hυ curve of N-GQDs (red trace). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Photoluminescence excitation (red line, λem = 415 nm), and emission spectra (green line, λex = 340 nm) of the N-GQDs dispersed in water at room temperature. (B) PL emission spectra at different excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 550 nm.
Synchrotron-based XPS spectra of the N-GQDs deposited in a thin film. All energies are adjusted to the fermi level energy, with E VB indicating the valence band cut-off energy for the film.
Proposed full band structures of N-GQDs.

head.ce:title: Band structures of blue luminescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots by synchrotron-based XPS

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper is dedicated to Professor P.R. Norton on the occasion of his 75th birthday, in honor of his profound contributions to Surface Science. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) attracted great interests attributed to their outstanding photoluminescence properties. Nitrogen doping is an effective strategy to engineer the optical and electron transfer properties of GQDs. Nitrogen doped GQDs (N-GQDs) were obtained by using cow milk as the precursor via a facile hydrothermal process with a Teflon-lined autoclave heated at 200 °C for 4.5 h. The purified N-GQDs were discovered to have high crystallinity with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to that of in-plane graphene (100 facet). The optical band gap of the N-GQDs was estimated from the Tauc plot derived from their UV–vis absorption spectrum. The full valence band spectrum of N-GQDs was collected and analyzed by means of synchrotron-based X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy for the first time. Corresponding full band structures were elucidated.

Bright blue luminescence GQDs with average size of 2.8 nm were prepared from cow milk by a facile hydrothermal process. Band structure of resulting GQDs was proposed from synchrotron based XPS and UV–vis absorption spectra for the first time.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Carbogenic quantum dots (CQDs) are novel luminescent nanomaterials that have sizes below 10 nm [1]. Like other semiconductors [2,3], CQDs show excellent optoelectronic properties. Moreover, CQDs possess other unique properties, such as strong luminescence, physiological stability, fine biocompatibility, solution processing ability, pH sensitivity, and low toxicity. CQDs are being exploited for biological applications, energy-related devices, catalysis, and water treatments [4–6]. CQDs can be divided into carbon quantum dots and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) according to the crystallinity of the core structure [5]. CQDs could be prepared from various precursors through “top-down” techniques [7,8] or by “bottom-up” approaches [9–11]. Among all the precursors, renewable sources are most preferred attributed to their low cost, nontoxicity and abundance [12].

Among the various attractive properties of GQDs, photoluminescence attracted the highest interest [4]. Heteroatom-doping emerged as a more general and convenient strategy to engineer the optical and electron transfer properties of GQDs [13]. Among various doping elements (including B, N, P, S, Se and Si), due to the facile doping process and a comparable atomic size of N to C atoms, N-doping of GQDs (N-GQDs) is the most adopted strategy to tune their luminescent properties [14].

In this work, N-GQDs were prepared from cow milk precursor via a facile hydrothermal process. Band gap of as prepared N-GQDs was estimated from Tauc plot derived from UV–vis absorption spectrum. The full valence band spectrum of N-GQDs was collected by synchrotron-based X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy for the first time. Corresponding full band structures are elucidated.
Cow milk (2%, partly skimmed, Sealtest) was bought from a local grocery store in London, Ontario. All other reagents were of analytical grades and used without further purification.

The hydrothermal process was adapted from reference [15] and optimized for preparation of N-GQDs. Briefly, 55 mL of milk was added into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 4.5 h. After the above hydrothermal reaction, the resulting suspension was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min to remove deep brown precipitate. The supernatant was further filtrated through a syringe filter with pore size of 100 nm. To remove the residual soluble byproducts, the filtrate was transferred into a dialysis bag (M w cut-off of 0.5–1 kDa) immersed in stirred bulk water for two days during which the water was changed 6 times. N-GQD powder was obtained by freeze-drying the solution after the above dialysis process.

The photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY) of N-GQDs was obtained by taking quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (a.q.) (quantum yield: 0.54 at 360 nm) as the reference [16]. Then UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra of N-GQDs aqueous solution (with 340 nm excitation) and the reference (with 360 nm excitation) were measured, respectively. The UV–vis absorption intensity was controlled under 0.1 in the 10 mm quartz cell to minimize the inner filter effect. The QY value was calculated according to the given equation:
Q
Y s
= Q
Y ref
×
I s
×
A ref
×
n
s
2
/
(
I ref
×
A s
×
n
ref
2
)
where QY means Quantum yield. Subscript “s” and “ref” refer to sample and reference respectively. I is the integrated emission intensity. A is the UV–vis absorption intensity at 360 nm for quinine sulfate and 340 nm for the sample. And n is the refractive index with 1.33 as the default for both quinine sulfate and carbon dots solvent (water) [16].

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images and the electron dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) result of the samples were obtained using a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 microscope. Certain amount of sample was dispersed in water under ultrasonication for approximately 10 min to yield a light yellow suspension with concentration around 1 mg/mL, and then the N-GQDs suspension was diluted to ∼10 µg/mL of which 6 µL suspension was dropcasted onto carbon coated copper grids and dried at ambient temperature. UV–vis absorption test was conducted on a Varian Cary 50 SCAN spectrometer, using a wavelength range of 800–200 nm and a scan rate of 1 nm/s. The photoluminescence spectra of N-GQD solution were collected on a fluorimeter, PTI Fluorolog QM-7/2005, with all slit widths of 0.5 mm. Synchrotron-based X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out at the Canadian light source (CLS) on the variable line spacing plane grating monochromater (VLS-PGM, 11ID-2) beamline. Measurements were taken at a flux > 1 × 1011 photons/s/100 mA on a 500 × 500 µm spot size. The workfunction was calibrated to the center of the fermi level in Au-foil. Incoming photons were set to 160 eV.

Milk, a very important source of nutrient, contains water, carbohydrates, fat, proteins, and a few amounts of minerals. Milk is a low cost product and could serve as both carbon and nitrogen sources to prepare N-GQDs as in the preparation of amphiphilic cow milk-derived carbon dots-supported silver nanoparticles [15]. In this work, luminescent N-GQDs were prepared from milk via a facile hydrothermal process. The yield of N-GQDs is about 5% taking 2% proteins in milk as 100%. As-prepared N-GQDs show good monodispersity as revealed from the TEM image in Fig. 1 A. High crystallinity of N-GQDs can be observed from the clear crystalline lattice fringes in the HRTEM image (inset in Fig. 1A). The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponds to that of in-plane graphene (100 facet) [9,17]. The size of N-GQDs distributes in a narrow range of 1.5–4.0 nm with an average size of 2.8 nm (Fig. 1B). Quantum yield (QY) of N-GQDs is determined to be 0.08 with quinine sulfate as the reference, which is in the high end of QYs reported by other groups [16,18,19]. The chemical compositions of C, N, O, P, S and Ca are 95.09%at, 0.95%at, 2.48%at, 0.04%at, 0.01%at and 0.11%at, respectively. The tiny Ca is from the milk as listed in its ingredient table. Note that an apparent layer of amorphous carbon shell (non-crystalline) was not observed. It is hard to tell whether N-GQDs possess core/shell structure from HRTEM images in Fig. 1A.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the N-GQDs (Fig. 2 A) shows a typical π→π* transition absorption peak corresponding to the aromatic sp2 domains around 217 nm, a n→π* transition absorption peak around 275 nm, a band-gap transition absorption peak around 333 nm and a long tail extending into the visible range. The solution of N-GQDs with a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL shows light yellow color under daylight but bright blue luminescence under the illumination of UV light at 365 nm as one can see in the insets of Fig. 2A. The corresponding optical band gap (E g) of a semiconductor could be estimated from the Tauc plot [i.e., the curve of (αhυ)r versus hυ converted from the UV–vis spectrum [20], in which α, h, and υ are the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, and light frequency, respectively, and r = 1/2 for an indirect band gap material and r = 2 for a direct band gap material] [21]. Fig. 2B shows a good linear fit when using r = 2, in accord with previous work [22,23] demonstrating a direct band gap of N-GQDs (no good linear fit is obtained for r = 1/2). The E g value of the N-GQDs was thus determined to be 3.35 eV by measuring the x-axis intercept of an extrapolated line from the linear regime of the curve (Fig. 2B, green curve). The Tauc plot of N-GQDs also shows an apparent tail between 2.50 and 3.30 eV, which is helpful for improving the light absorbance [21]. The observed band gap of the prepared N-GQDs, showing an intrinsic semiconductor like absorption, occurs in the blue optical region [20]. Interestingly, additional energy gaps were identified in the Tauc plot. These result from the excitation of non-bonding electrons in the dopant atoms—N, O, S, and P—as an n–π* transition, and the sp2 electrons in the C atoms of the graphene structure in a π–π* [24]. The presence of the n–π* transition confirms the intercalation of the dopants into the graphene lattice. These atoms act as electron donors into the band structure, raising the fermi energy relative to un-doped GQDs, and lowering the effective π* level [22]. There is also an apparent inductive effect which results from the higher electronegativity of the most common dopants atoms, N and O. These atoms inductively draw electrons out of the π orbitals, a process facilitated by the high conjugation between carbons. The lowered electron density in the π orbitals will decrease that energy level, creating a much larger π–π* gap. The n–π* gap fits between the HOMO and the π orbitals, raising the energy on the HOMO and decreasing the band gap. This creates a complicated interaction between the nitrogen and oxygen components within the GQDs, and when coupled with the size distribution, leads to the broadness as identified in Fig. 2A, and highlights the tunability of the band gap [25,26].

Photoluminescence spectra (Fig. 3 A) of the N-GQDs solution indicate a maximum excitation wavelength at 340 nm and maximum emission wavelength at 415 nm. Very interestingly, the emission spectra display a typical excitation wavelength-dependent characteristic: the emissive wavelength is red-shifted under the excitation with longer wavelengths (Fig. 3B). These results are very similar to previously reported CQDs [18,27]. When the excitation wavelength changes from 300 to 550 nm, the PL peak correspondingly shifts from 409 to 580 nm. The tunable emission of the N-GQDs have been suggested to be a result of varied photoluminescence characteristics of CQDs in different sizes or the existence of different emissive sites on the surfaces of the CQDs [27]. However, the exact mechanism accounting for the excitation wavelength-dependent emission remains to be established.

The full valence band (VB) spectrum was measured through synchrotron-based XPS for the N-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 4 . The valence band energy (E VB) onset occurs at 2.67 eV from the fermi level. Combined with the information from the Tauc plot (Fig. 2B), there is a 0.67 eV gap between the fermi level and the conduction band energy (E CB). The fermi level is significantly closer to the conduction band (CB), as expected for any n-type semiconductor. The small gap between the fermi level and CB gives rise to the tailing structure seen in the very low binding energy between the fermi level and the onset of the CB in the spectrum. This further explains why the tailing noted in the Tauc plot occurs at the band gap [22,28]. Peak 1 (Fig. 4) shows highly irregular shape due to the contributions from multiple electron transitions. As predicted by UV–vis, peak 1 is comprised of the non-bonding electrons from both oxygen and nitrogen, as well as the 2p σ-electrons that make up the carbon-carbon lattice. These are the highest energy electrons in the system, and are the primary contributors to the observable excitation. This means that the light emission of the N-GQDs is primarily dependent on the mixing of the dopant atoms, and the available non-bonding electrons on the dopant atoms, with the CC backbone. Tuning the valence band energy will therefore be accomplished through dopant non-bonding electrons of various energies to cause changes in the VB onset, in a similar way to surface modification in quantum dots [25]. Furthermore, the combination of these peaks leads to the broadness observed in peak 1. Peak 2 arises from the CO σ-bonding. These bonds are slightly lower-lying than the CC bonds due to the lower average bond energy, but have a higher bond energy than the CN bonds. The CO σ-bonds are situated at about 11.1 eV, which is similar to the energy seen in literature [29]. These bonds are less influential on the immediate emission processes; however, they appear to shift based on the strength of the electron withdrawing effects, both polar and inductively. The presence of the electronegative oxygen atom will draw electrons from the C center. This creates the higher-energy oxygen non-bonding electron orbitals, which then cause a decrease in the energy associated with the CO σ-bond. These two deviations balance each other out relative to the un-doped GQD. Interactions of this nature are common among different electron-donating dopants, and have been observed in a variety of different species, and atoms. [28,30]. The CN σ-bond has a lower energy relative to the CO bond, but would still exhibit similar splitting of the non-bonding and sp2-bound electrons. The reduced energy of the CN σ-bonds relative to those of CO also signifies that these bonds will appear further down the spectrum. In Fig. 4, the CN bonds show as the very small absorption around 17 eV (peak 3). These peaks are consistent within literature, yet are far less pronounced in the N-GQDs measured herein [31] due to its low content (<0.95%at from EDS results). Both peak 2 and peak 3 arise from highly similar transitions, yet the intensity of peak 2 is around three times that of peak 3. A higher peak 2 intensity confirms that there is greater contribution to the valence band from CO σ-bonds than CN, strongly suggesting that the ratio of O:N dopant present in the sample is greater than 3 (estimated from the integrated intensity of peak 2 and 3). The presence of the deeper-level O 2s electrons at peak 4 further supports the notion of greater oxygen content relative to nitrogen, especially given the lack of N 2s peaks in the mid-30 eV region. Literature sources also suggest that significant nitrogen doping would lead to an enhanced signal from the non-bonding electrons, adding features to peak 1 [24,28]. Interestingly, peak 5 does not correspond to any significant carbon or dopant peak. Rather, it comes about from the 3p orbitals on Ca. The presence of Ca within the N-GQDs appears to be a byproduct of using milk to synthesize the dots, possibly leading to some Ca intercalation within the structure [28]. The presence of Ca was confirmed through EDS. The Ca presence results in a cationic metal dopant intercalating in the N-GQDs. This works contrary to n-type dopants discussed previously, and its presence in the N-GQDs and the consequently derived films require further study to elucidate the effects separate of the n-type dopants. Despite this, the location of the energy level is significantly below the VB, and as such can be treated as an interesting component of the VB, but as having little to no effect on the actual energy gap of in the materials.

From the VB structure and the UV–vis data, full band structures are elucidated for these N-GQDs in Fig. 5 . From the figure, we can identify the primary contributions from the VB relative to each other, and identify potential charge-trapping sites, as well as key strengths to these N-GQDs for constructing film-based devices. The wider band gap presents a challenge for overcoming the initial barriers to charge-excitation, though the presence of the conduction band near the fermi level makes for a fairly strong n-type material [32]. The presence of the various lower-energy orbitals in the VB provides ideal methods for tuning the VBmax [25]. This provides a good basis from which to design films for optoelectronic devices.

Bright blue luminescence N-GQDs with an average size of 2.8 nm were obtained from cow milk. Quantum yield of N-GQDs is calculated to be 0.08 with quinine sulfate as the reference. Tauc plots indicated as prepared N-GQDs to be direct band gap material and optical band gap was estimated to be 3.35 eV. The emission spectra of N-GQDs solution display a typical excitation wavelength-dependent characteristic, and the emissive wavelength is red-shifted under excitation with longer wavelengths. The full valence band spectrum of the N-GQDs was collected through synchrotron-based XPS. The onset valence band energy occurs at 2.67 eV from the fermi level. Full band structures are elucidated for these N-GQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC, DG RGPIN-2013-201697 and SPG STPGP-2016-493924), Canada Foundation for Innovation/Ontario Innovation Trust (CFI/OIT, 9040), Premier's Research Excellence Award (PREA, 2003), and The University of Western Ontario. We appreciate the beam times provided by the Canadian Light Source (CLS), help of the facilities and beamline scientists, and, in particular, guidance of Dr. Xiaoyu Cui throughout the experimentation. We would like to thank the Electronic Shop, Glass Shop and ChemBio Store in the Department of Chemistry for their quality service. We are also grateful to Dr. Zhiqiang Wang for his assistance prior to our CLS visit, and Dr. T.K. Sham for his long-term guidance with regards to all things on synchrotron.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.susc.2018.01.013.
